Architecture

Competition · Volume Zero · Team · 2022

Acarya — Tiny Library

A competition entry for the Volume Zero Tiny Library competition. Sited in Googal, Raichur, Karnataka — a village that has remained largely unknown to the world — the library is designed as a temple of knowledge drawing on local Vachana and Janapada cultural traditions. The structure is lifted on stilts for flood resilience, creating multipurpose space below while achieving an angular river view from above.

CompetitionPublic SpaceLibrary Cultural HeritageFlood ResilienceVernacular
SiteGoogal, Raichur, Karnataka
Year2022
CompetitionVolume Zero · Team Entry
Team CodeVZTL25ED16
TeamAnusha Jambaldinni · Rakshitha Javagal · Monish Datta

Concept — temple of knowledge · Site: Googal, Raichur · Form development sequence · Zoning strategy · Render

Concept

A Temple of Knowledge

The village of Googal has a temple of Vachanakara Allamaprabhu — the Acarya (spiritual master). The library exacts this same by making the library a temple of knowledge with modern insight. The village's rich cultural history is promoted through the traditional forms of learning: Vachanas — rhythmic writing — and Janapada — folk tradition where knowledge is passed on through songs, poetry and art.

Flood Resilience

Site is flood-prone. Level of massing is raised as a precaution for floods. Higher elevation achieved by stilts, also allowing greater accessibility, natural ventilation and an angular river view.

Form Logic

Extrusion of massing allows greater accessibility and ventilation. Extruded mass is reused as a courtyard. Symmetry references the colonnaded temple — traditional structures of India — as direct inspiration.

Zoning

Spaces zoned according to accessibility, age and purpose in a linear arrangement: Kids zone, Common areas, Public areas, Areas of solitude, Tech room — different levels of massing provide a sense of direction.

Plans, Sections & Interiors

Jaali, Arches & Traditional Materials

Jaali work is done on facades to diffuse harsh sunlight and keep the room cool by continuous ventilation — economical and aesthetically pleasing. Roofs are spanned by jack arches to imitate caves. The balconies opening to the riverside have doors which pivot to make a wooden door or a book rack accessible on both sides. Materials are chosen considering location, availability and economy: clay blocks (lightweight, locally available), RCC frame, timber, glass and polycarbonate sheets from recycled waste.
